critic_raw:
The stratified random split across 60 months is the central methodological problem. Hospital coding practices, clinical note writing conventions, discharge planning policies, and patient population composition all shift over a 60-month window. Mixing patient encounters from year 1 and year 5 in both training and test sets means the model is not evaluated on its ability to generalize to future patients — it is evaluated on patients from the same time period as training, after random shuffling. This inflates estimated performance for the clinical BERT-based model particularly, because text styles and clinical vocabulary evolve over time and random mixing makes the test set look like the training set.

The feature set is not comparable between models: the multi-task deep learning model receives structured features plus clinical notes (via TF-IDF and clinical BERT), while the logistic regression baseline receives only structured features. Any performance gain attributed to the multi-task architecture could be entirely due to clinical text access rather than model design. The hypothesis is about multi-task learning improving prediction; the experiment tests multi-modal feature access, which is a different question.

The auxiliary task predicts length-of-stay using shared early layers. LOS at discharge is a feature available at prediction time for a post-discharge readmission model. If LOS is included in the structured feature set (it is listed), then the auxiliary task is predicting a value already given as input to both models. This creates a degenerate auxiliary task that learns to reconstruct an input rather than providing meaningful regularization.

Preprocessing transformers are fit on training data only for the structured features (correctly stated in Section 3), but the clinical BERT model is described as pre-trained and fine-tuned — the fine-tuning step would use training data correctly. However, the TF-IDF vocabulary is built on training data, which is correctly done. The main leakage concern is the split strategy, not preprocessing.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split over 60 months ignores temporal distribution shift in clinical coding, documentation practices, and patient population — test performance is evaluated against patients from the same mixed time distribution as training, not future patients as required for clinical deployment validity.

The multi-task deep learning model receives clinical text (TF-IDF + clinical BERT) while the logistic regression baseline receives only structured features — any observed performance difference cannot be attributed to multi-task architecture versus single-task because the models also differ in feature access.

Length-of-stay is both an input structured feature and the target of the auxiliary prediction task — predicting a value that is also given as input to both models creates a degenerate auxiliary objective that reconstructs inputs rather than learning regularizing representations.

AUROC as primary metric in a 15% positive-class setting (typical 30-day readmission rate) will show inflated separation between models even when the clinical utility difference is marginal; AUPRC or net reclassification index would be more discriminating for comparing high-performing models.
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split over 60 months ignores temporal distribution shift in clinical coding, documentation practices, and patient population — test performance is evaluated against patients from the same mixed time distribution as training, not future patients as required for clinical deployment validity.

The multi-task deep learning model receives clinical text (TF-IDF + clinical BERT) while the logistic regression baseline receives only structured features — any observed performance difference cannot be attributed to multi-task architecture versus single-task because the models also differ in feature access.

Length-of-stay is both an input structured feature and the target of the auxiliary prediction task — predicting a value that is also given as input to both models creates a degenerate auxiliary objective that reconstructs inputs rather than learning regularizing representations.

AUROC as primary metric in a 15% positive-class setting (typical 30-day readmission rate) will show inflated separation between models even when the clinical utility difference is marginal; AUPRC or net reclassification index would be more discriminating for comparing high-performing models.
